title: Sort an Array
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: All three proposals are genuinely distinct here, one per axis: counting sort (speed, exploits the bounded value range), introsort (balanced default), in-place heapsort (minimum memory).

variants.languageReason: std::sort is introsort with an inlined comparator: fast in practice on any input, O(log n) stack, no assumption about the value range. Best general balance of speed and memory.
A tight native counting-sort loop is comparison-free and linear; the bounded value range (R = 100001) makes it the raw-speed winner.
variants.approachJustification: Counting sort: tally occurrences over the offset range, then emit values in order.

variants.languageReason: Smallest runtime/stdlib baseline among the allowed languages; manual control enables a truly in-place, allocation-free heapsort.
variants.approachJustification: In-place heapsort with an iterative sift-down (no auxiliary array, no recursion stack).

languageReason: See variants[].languageReason. Recommended default is solution.cpp (C++ introsort): best practical runtime/memory balance.
reasoningSummary: Comparison sorts are bounded by O(n log n), but the small fixed value range lets counting sort beat that on raw speed at O(R) memory, while in-place heapsort reaches O(1) extra memory at the cost of the log factor. Introsort is the robust middle and the default.
approachJustification: Three non-dominated points: counting sort (speed), introsort (balance), heapsort (memory).
performanceStrategy: Counting sort = comparison-free linear scan (speed); in-place iterative heapsort = zero allocation (memory); std::sort = tuned introsort (balance).
timeComplexity: O(n log n) recommended / O(n + R) (counting) / O(n log n) (heapsort)
spaceComplexity: O(log n) recommended / O(R) (counting) / O(1) extra (heapsort)
